analysis:
- Top performers share a clear structure: Pareto prefilter → surrogate scoring (rect-volume ± diversity) → adaptive shortlist (c·k) → lazy/stamped greedy heap → exact HV only for popped items → bounded local single-swap refinement. This balances quality vs cost.
- Common cost-saving patterns: stamping/lazy heap to avoid recomputing gains, hv result caching, using pygmo when available, and Monte‑Carlo fallbacks. Stronger heuristics also adapt shortlist size to k and prioritize high-rect survivors so HV calls focus on promising points.
- Efficiency vs memory tradeoffs: several variants precompute a global MC dominated-mask (fast marginal counts) which dramatically speeds marginal evaluations but can be memory-heavy (M×N booleans). Others reuse shared MC samples instead of per-candidate random draws—this is a big win for reproducibility and speed.
- Avoidable overheads seen in weaker ones: naive pairwise nondomination loops, repeated array copies/stacking for every HV call, frequent creation of frozensets/tuples as cache keys, and recomputing full HV from scratch instead of incrementally using cached dominated samples or stamping. Some designs compute expensive diversity (full pairwise distances) even when m is large.
- Quality-controls: diversity blending (distance-to-sample/centroid/min-dist) helps final spread but needs capped cost; surrogate hyper-parameters (alpha/beta/gamma, pool size) greatly affect search breadth. Local swaps improve solutions but should be bounded and prioritized by surrogate gains to avoid many HV calls.
- Robustness: heuristics that fallback cleanly (fill-by-rect, early stop when samples fully covered) and that cap MC M by D/k perform better across instances.
experience: Prefer a small, adaptive shortlist seeded by a blended surrogate, reuse a shared MC sample mask for marginal counts, and run a stamped lazy greedy that only exact-evaluates heap tops with a cached HV API (pygmo). Avoid full pairwise loops and per-call array rebuilds. Limit swap searches by surrogate rank and use incremental sample-based deltas to approve expensive HV recomputations.
current_reflection: Prefer a small, adaptive shortlist seeded by a blended surrogate, reuse a shared MC sample mask for marginal counts, and run a stamped lazy greedy that only exact-evaluates heap tops with a cached HV API (pygmo). Avoid full pairwise loops and per-call array rebuilds. Limit swap searches by surrogate rank and use incremental sample-based deltas to approve expensive HV recomputations.
good_reflections: Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.

Keep the stamped lazy-greedy backbone: Pareto prefilter → adaptive rect‑surrogate shortlist → stamped heap + HV-cache → tiny bounded swap. Map original indices into a compact pool, compute HVs with pygmo when available, otherwise reuse a small shared MC sample for marginal estimates. Limit pairwise/diversity costs and cap shortlist/swap budgets adaptively by k to maximize HV per compute.

Seed a stamped lazy-greedy heap with normalized singleton rect scores, keep a compact shortlist ∝ k, cache HVs using canonical sorted tuples or bitmasks, and recompute exact marginal only for popped items. Reuse Monte‑Carlo dominated masks (bitsets) to compute marginals incrementally; adapt sample size to D and pool size. Limit and prioritize swap trials. Profile hotspots and avoid O(N^2) Python loops—vectorize or chunk.

Prefilter by Pareto, build an adaptive shortlist using a blended surrogate (rect volume + normalized shared global MC dominated counts + a lightweight diversity term), then run a stamped lazy greedy heap that recomputes true gains only at the top (use pgm exact HV when available). Cache bitset dominated masks and HV results, use a tiny focused swap pool, vectorize nondomination, and adapt sample/pool sizes to N, D, k for speed and reliable marginal estimates.
bad_reflections: Favor a lazy‑greedy driven by cheap, precomputed Monte‑Carlo bitsets for fast marginal estimates and only call exact HV for the top ranked candidates. Shortlist adaptively (c·k), use vectorized nondominated filtering and bitset OR/count operations to evaluate gains, and keep caching lightweight. This yields near‑exact selections with minimal expensive HV calls and far lower Python overhead.

Next gen heuristics should combine Pareto prefilter → rect/diversity shortlist → stamped lazy greedy seeded by surrogate, but compute marginals from precomputed bitsets of shared Monte‑Carlo samples (or use pygmo for low D). Limit shortlist size adaptively, cap local swaps, and avoid repeated tuple/frozenset constructions; these choices give near‑optimal HV with much lower runtime.

Favor a stamped lazy‑greedy seeded by a blended surrogate, keep shortlist proportional to k, reuse a shared MC dominated mask for fast marginals, cache HVs with sorted‑tuple keys, adapt MC sample size to D/k, and limit local swaps to top outsiders. Vectorize nondomination and avoid Python-level loops in hot paths.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: stamped lazy‑greedy, adaptive surrogate shortlist, MC bitsets, pygmo HV, HV cache, incremental deltas, bounded local swap, vectorize nondomination.
- Advice: Pareto‑prefilter, seed a compact shortlist (c·k) with a blended surrogate (rect + MC dominated counts + diversity), run a stamped lazy heap, exact‑evaluate marginals only for popped tops (pygmo when available), reuse shared MC sample masks and cached HVs (sorted tuple/bitmask keys), and allow tiny bounded swaps guided by surrogate rank.
- Avoid: full pairwise loops, per‑call array/frozenset rebuilds, repeated HV calls on same sets, unbounded swap/search, and Python O(N^2) hot paths.
- Explanation: minimizes expensive HV computations, amortizes Monte‑Carlo effort, enables vectorized nondomination and incremental marginals, yielding near‑optimal HV per compute.
